(Tradition) Before the Nara period, the main shrine was rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} period (770-780) |Priest priest = Makichiyo Ouchi |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}Copper plate roof |Alternative name = Great Water Lord Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = Sanuki 15 shrines (No. 2) |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 18th|Q2521|WD=Q2521}} (Spring Regular Festival/Agricultural Tools Market) |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Mizunushi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} ``Mizushi Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454}}{{ill|lt=Higashikagawa City|Q861255|WD=Q861255}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wataridayi Hyakushohime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and the old {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Shakaku}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|lt=Sanukikuni|Q929682|WD=Q929682}}One of the 24 Shikinai shrines. === Enshrined God === ; Main enshrined deity * {{ill|lt=Watari-to-hi-momo-sou-hime-no-mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098|lt='''Watari-to-hi-momo-so-hime no mikoto'''}} ; precinct shrine * Korei Shrine - {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (Korei Tennou) Behind the main shrine, the father of the enshrined deity. * Kunidama Shrine - {{ill|lt=Wakoku Kahime|Q97539246|WD=Q97539246|lt=Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto}} (Yamato no Kunikahime no Mikoto) Right hand side of the main hall, mother of the enshrined deity. * Mizushu Sanzan (Kumano Sansha) Mountain worship It is said that Sojo Soun will be transferring the shrine. ** Shingu Shrine (Toramaruyama) - {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami Mikoto}} (Izanami Mikoto) ** Hongu Shrine (Mt. Hongu) - {{ill|lt=Hayatama no Omikoto|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Hayatama no Mikoto}} (Hayatama no Mikoto) ** Nachi Shrine (Mt. Nachi) - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=jikaiotokoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Kotosakao no Mikoto) * Water Shrine - {{jalink|Mizuhanome|Q8191796|Mizuhanome no Mikoto} (Mizuhanome no Mikoto) Also known as the Elephant Goddess or Seoritsuhime.* Daisui-dera Temple (founding date unknown) Suishinushi Shrine Bettoji Temple. The principal image is Amida Nyorai (to Enko-ji Temple), Fudo Myoo, Nidoji (to Yoda-ji Temple), and Eleven-faced Kannon (to Sakate/Kannon-ji Temple). Suitokusan Hojuin Jingu-ji Temple was changed to Daisui-dera Temple during the Kanbun year. '''{{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}''', Enreki 9th year (at the age of 24), visited Suishupu Shrine and Daisui-dera Temple. '''{{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}}''' dug the Aka well water in the precincts and dedicated it to the shrine (currently the well of Yegadani). Until the end of the Edo period, Shintoism and Buddhism were mixed, and in March of the first year of the Meiji era, an order was issued to separate Shintoism and Buddhism, and on February 25, 1887, the temple was officially closed down. === Overview === ; Outline It is located on the right bank of the Yoda River, at the foot of a mountain near Ouchi Dam. It is also called Omizusushi Daimyojin. The shrine bush behind the shrine is a natural environment conservation area for Kagawa Prefecture, and the Inori cedar within the precincts has been designated as a preserved tree by the prefecture. Many stone tools from the Jomon period and earthenware from the Yayoi and Kofun periods have been discovered in the vicinity, and there is also an ancient tomb on top of the mountain that is said to be the mausoleum of a princess, and the treasure trove contains many cultural assets. All of the shrine buildings are unified in {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|lt=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the standing stone marking the shrine's territory still remains in Ouchi and Shiratori-cho. According to the shrine's legend, the enshrined deity, Hyakushohime Wakatsubi, left the Kuroda Palace of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} when she was 8 years old, and was washed ashore here on a {{ill|lt=Tenban ship|Q126914107|WD=Q126914107|lt=Utsubo boat}}. Hyakushohime was excellent at predicting the future, and she made it rain for people suffering from sunlight, taught them about water sources, opened waterways, and helped them grow rice. There is a mausoleum on the mountain behind the shrine. ; Shrine{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=Mizunushi Shrine |publisher=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1227836/1/96 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}* {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa}}3rd year (836) November 1st Jinshin (7th) Article "Sanuki Province Water Lord Deity Ceremony Consecrated to Jugoi" {{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1194459/1/56 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}) * {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} On April 9, 866, Mijo Mijo, the water lord appears to be the ``Junior Fifth Rank Sewer Lord God of Jusanuki Province''{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/991094/1/105 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Japanese Three Dynasties True Story|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|lt=Three Generations True Story}}) * {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 3rd year (940): After praying for the pacification of the pirates, he was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi, and was further promoted to the rank of Shoichi ({{ill|lt=Chokan Kanbun|Q11651695|WD=Q11651695}}) * {{ill|lt=Koyasu|Q1327182|WD=Q1327182}}4th year (967) "Sanuki Province {{ill|lt=Ouchi District|Q31478|WD=Q31478}} Mizusushi Shrine" ({{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}})* At the time of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 (1872) {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, it was promoted to {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} * Promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} on January 23, 1903 * On March 22, 1907, designated as {{ill|lt=Shinsenheihakuryokushinsha|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Shinenheihakuryokushin Shrine}} ; History * The reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (around 290 BC|Q28200}}) Kuroda {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Andomiya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Watakutenhi Hyakusōhime no Mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098}} settled in Suishunushi at the age of 8 and was praised as the god of free water virtue. * {{ill|lt=Emperor Bunmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} 3rd year (699) {{ill|lt=Dainagon|Q1354706|WD=Q1354706}}{{ill|lt=Ishigamimaro|Q6086140|WD=Q6086140}} dedicates {{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} to our company and prays for the end of the epidemic * {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}}1st year to 12th year (770-780) Reconstruction of the Gotare ruins * {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}9 (790) Dengyo Daishi, {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}, at the age of 24, visited Suishinushi Shrine and Daisui Temple.* Around {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} (810-824), Kobo Daishi {{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}} dug a well of water in the grounds of Suishinushi Shrine and dedicated it to Suishinushi Shrine (it still exists in the grounds as the Well of Suishinushi). * {{ill|lt=Tenryaku|Q1346281|WD=Q1346281}} 2nd year (948) {{ill|lt=Doctor of Literature|Q11500074|WD=Q11500074}} Tachibana Naoki goes as {{ill|lt=Imperial Envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920}} * {{ill|lt=Owa|Q1324768|WD=Q1324768}}2nd year (962){{ill|lt=Council|Q1133663|WD=Q1133663}}{{ill|lt=Tadasuke Fujiwara|Q11623 263|WD=Q11623 263|lt=Fujiwara Asaomi Tadasuke}} worshiped the {{ill|lt=Imperial edict|Q905765|WD=Q905765}} and prayed for rain, and there was a proclamation, and the manor was called Ōsuinushi, and the name was called Ōsuinushi Taisha. * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}} 1st year (1131) Visit of Sanuki {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Tsunetaka Fujiwara|Q134963520|WD=Q134963520}}* {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}}2 years (1132) {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} which was 2 towns, 2 tans and 60 feet became 24 towns and 5 tans. Around this time, there are records that {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Mokudai|Q11581544|WD=Q11581544}} frequently visited the shrine. It is believed that {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} was donated in the form of dividing a part of Yoda-go and Irino-go, which were Garyo|Q11421344}}. {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367}} It reached the peak of prosperity with 75 members and 42 {{ill|lt=Sōbo|Q11387615|WD=Q11387615|lt=Sōbo}}. assets === === Important cultural property === *7 wooden statues of godsThe history of this item's designation as a cultural property is as follows. *In 1901 (Meiji 34), the following three buildings (total of eight buildings) were designated as important cultural properties (formerly national treasures). (Ministry of Home Affairs Notification No. 20, March 27, 1909) **Three wooden statues of the gods (Seated statue of Yamatobi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto, Seated statue of Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto, and Seated statue of Owa Nekohikotaikion no Mikoto) **1 wooden seated male god statue **4 wooden seated goddess statues *Of the ``Three Wooden God Statues'', the Watatsubi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto statue was purchased by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2017 after its whereabouts were unknown for a while. *In 2020 (Reiwa 2), the ``Three Wooden God Statues'' were divided into two: ``Seated Statue of Watanbi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto'' (Kept by the Agency for Cultural Affairs) and ``Seated Statue of Wakoku Kaohime no Mikoto/Owa Nekohiko Taisho Hime no Mikoto'' (Owned by Mizunushi Shrine). The latter was combined with two previously designated Important Cultural Properties (``1 Wooden Seated Statue of a Male God'' and ``4 Wooden Seated Statues of a Goddess'') and was designated as an Important Cultural Property under the name ``7 Wooden God Statues''. (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 120, September 30, 2020) *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Raimon Radenkura *{{ill|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra|Q15905785|WD=Q15905785|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra}} 600 chapters included: 60 sutra boxes, inner bottom sutra box promotion record, etc. in ink Authority: For properties designated up to 2000, refer to "National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties, Special Volume," Comprehensive Catalog by Owner, Comprehensive Name Index, and Statistical Materials (Mainichi Shimbun, 2000). Changes since 2001 are noted separately. === Tangible Cultural Property designated by Kagawa Prefecture === *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Wooden lion head dedicated in the 5th year of Bun'an era Authority: {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|Q11667347|WD=Q11667347}} Site{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/kenkyoui/bunka/culturalassets.html |title=Cultural assets of Kagawa|publisher=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|accessdate=20209-01-05 }} === Access === About 10 minutes by car from the Shiratoriouchi Interchange on the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Expressway|Q7677132|WD=Q7677132}}. (Tradition) Before the Nara period, the main shrine was rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} period (770-780) |Priest priest = Makichiyo Ouchi |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}Copper plate roof |Alternative name = Great Water Lord Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = Sanuki 15 shrines (No. 2) |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 18th|Q2521|WD=Q2521}} (Spring Regular Festival/Agricultural Tools Market) |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Mizunushi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} ``Mizushi Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454}}{{ill|lt=Higashikagawa City|Q861255|WD=Q861255}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wataridayi Hyakushohime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and the old {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Shakaku}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|lt=Sanukikuni|Q929682|WD=Q929682}}One of the 24 Shikinai shrines. === Enshrined God === ; Main enshrined deity * {{ill|lt=Watari-to-hi-momo-sou-hime-no-mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098|lt='''Watari-to-hi-momo-so-hime no mikoto'''}} ; precinct shrine * Korei Shrine - {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (Korei Tennou) Behind the main shrine, the father of the enshrined deity. * Kunidama Shrine - {{ill|lt=Wakoku Kahime|Q97539246|WD=Q97539246|lt=Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto}} (Yamato no Kunikahime no Mikoto) Right hand side of the main hall, mother of the enshrined deity. * Mizushu Sanzan (Kumano Sansha) Mountain worship It is said that Sojo Soun will be transferring the shrine. ** Shingu Shrine (Toramaruyama) - {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami Mikoto}} (Izanami Mikoto) ** Hongu Shrine (Mt. Hongu) - {{ill|lt=Hayatama no Omikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatama no Mikoto}} (Hayatama no Mikoto) ** Nachi Shrine (Mt. Nachi) - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=jikaiotokoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Kotosakao no Mikoto) * Water Shrine - {{jalink|Mizuhanome|Q8191796|Mizuhanome no Mikoto} (Mizuhanome no Mikoto) Also known as the Elephant Goddess or Seoritsuhime.* Daisui-dera Temple (founding date unknown) Suishinushi Shrine Bettoji Temple. The principal image is Amida Nyorai (to Enko-ji Temple), Fudo Myoo, Nidoji (to Yoda-ji Temple), and Eleven-faced Kannon (to Sakate/Kannon-ji Temple). Suitokusan Hojuin Jingu-ji Temple was changed to Daisui-dera Temple during the Kanbun year. '''{{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}''', Enreki 9th year (at the age of 24), visited Suishupu Shrine and Daisui-dera Temple. '''{{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}}''' dug the Aka well water in the precincts and dedicated it to the shrine (currently the well of Yegadani). Until the end of the Edo period, Shintoism and Buddhism were mixed, and in March of the first year of the Meiji era, an order was issued to separate Shintoism and Buddhism, and on February 25, 1887, the temple was officially closed down. === Overview === ; Outline It is located on the right bank of the Yoda River, at the foot of a mountain near Ouchi Dam. It is also called Omizusushi Daimyojin. The shrine bush behind the shrine is a natural environment conservation area for Kagawa Prefecture, and the Inori cedar within the precincts has been designated as a preserved tree by the prefecture. Many stone tools from the Jomon period and earthenware from the Yayoi and Kofun periods have been discovered in the vicinity, and there is also an ancient tomb on top of the mountain that is said to be the mausoleum of a princess, and the treasure trove contains many cultural assets. All of the shrine buildings are unified in {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|lt=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the standing stone marking the shrine's territory still remains in Ouchi and Shiratori-cho. According to the shrine's legend, the enshrined deity, Hyakushohime Wakatsubi, left the Kuroda Palace of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} when she was 8 years old, and was washed ashore here on a {{ill|lt=Tenban ship|Q126914107|WD=Q126914107|lt=Utsubo boat}}. Hyakushohime was excellent at predicting the future, and she made it rain for people suffering from sunlight, taught them about water sources, opened waterways, and helped them grow rice. There is a mausoleum on the mountain behind the shrine. ; Shrine{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=Mizunushi Shrine |publisher=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1227836/1/96 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}* {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa}}3rd year (836) November 1st Jinshin (7th) Article "Sanuki Province Water Lord Deity Ceremony Consecrated to Jugoi" {{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1194459/1/56 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}) * {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} On April 9, 866, Mijo Mijo, the water lord appears to be the ``Junior Fifth Rank Sewer Lord God of Jusanuki Province''{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/991094/1/105 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Japanese Three Dynasties True Story|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|lt=Three Generations True Story}}) * {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 3rd year (940): After praying for the pacification of the pirates, he was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi, and was further promoted to the rank of Shoichi ({{ill|lt=Chokan Kanbun|Q11651695|WD=Q11651695}}) * {{ill|lt=Koyasu|Q1327182|WD=Q1327182}}4th year (967) "Sanuki Province {{ill|lt=Ouchi District|Q31478|WD=Q31478}} Mizusushi Shrine" ({{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}})* At the time of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 (1872) {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, it was promoted to {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} * Promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} on January 23, 1903 * On March 22, 1907, designated as {{ill|lt=Shinsenheihakuryokushinsha|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinenheihakuryokushin Shrine}} ; History * The reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (around 290 BC|Q28200}}) Kuroda {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Andomiya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Watakutenhi Hyakusōhime no Mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098}} settled in Suishunushi at the age of 8 and was praised as the god of free water virtue. * {{ill|lt=Emperor Bunmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} 3rd year (699) {{ill|lt=Dainagon|Q1354706|WD=Q1354706}}{{ill|lt=Ishigamimaro|Q6086140|WD=Q6086140}} dedicates {{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} to our company and prays for the end of the epidemic * {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}}1st year to 12th year (770-780) Reconstruction of the Gotare ruins * {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}9 (790) Dengyo Daishi, {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}, at the age of 24, visited Suishinushi Shrine and Daisui Temple.* Around {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} (810-824), Kobo Daishi {{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}} dug a well of water in the grounds of Suishinushi Shrine and dedicated it to Suishinushi Shrine (it still exists in the grounds as the Well of Suishinushi). * {{ill|lt=Tenryaku|Q1346281|WD=Q1346281}} 2nd year (948) {{ill|lt=Doctor of Literature|Q11500074|WD=Q11500074}} Tachibana Naoki goes as {{ill|lt=Imperial Envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920}} * {{ill|lt=Owa|Q1324768|WD=Q1324768}}2nd year (962){{ill|lt=Council|Q1133663|WD=Q1133663}}{{ill|lt=Tadasuke Fujiwara|Q11623 263|WD=Q11623 263|lt=Fujiwara Asaomi Tadasuke}} worshiped the {{ill|lt=Imperial edict|Q905765|WD=Q905765}} and prayed for rain, and there was a proclamation, and the manor was called Ōsuinushi, and the name was called Ōsuinushi Taisha. * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}} 1st year (1131) Visit of Sanuki {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Tsunetaka Fujiwara|Q134963520|WD=Q134963520}}* {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}}2 years (1132) {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} which was 2 towns, 2 tans and 60 feet became 24 towns and 5 tans. Around this time, there are records that {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Mokudai|Q11581544|WD=Q11581544}} frequently visited the shrine. It is believed that {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} was donated in the form of dividing a part of Yoda-go and Irino-go, which were Garyo|Q11421344}}. {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367}} It reached the peak of prosperity with 75 members and 42 {{ill|lt=Sōbo|Q11387615|WD=Q11387615|lt=Sōbo}}. assets === === Important cultural property === *7 wooden statues of godsThe history of this item's designation as a cultural property is as follows. *In 1901 (Meiji 34), the following three buildings (total of eight buildings) were designated as important cultural properties (formerly national treasures). (Ministry of Home Affairs Notification No. 20, March 27, 1909) **Three wooden statues of the gods (Seated statue of Yamatobi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto, Seated statue of Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto, and Seated statue of Owa Nekohikotaikion no Mikoto) **1 wooden seated male god statue **4 wooden seated goddess statues *Of the ``Three Wooden God Statues'', the Watatsubi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto statue was purchased by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2017 after its whereabouts were unknown for a while. *In 2020 (Reiwa 2), the ``Three Wooden God Statues'' were divided into two: ``Seated Statue of Watanbi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto'' (Kept by the Agency for Cultural Affairs) and ``Seated Statue of Wakoku Kaohime no Mikoto/Owa Nekohiko Taisho Hime no Mikoto'' (Owned by Mizunushi Shrine). The latter was combined with two previously designated Important Cultural Properties (``1 Wooden Seated Statue of a Male God'' and ``4 Wooden Seated Statues of a Goddess'') and was designated as an Important Cultural Property under the name ``7 Wooden God Statues''. (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 120, September 30, 2020) *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Raimon Radenkura *{{ill|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra|Q15905785|WD=Q15905785|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra}} 600 chapters included: 60 sutra boxes, inner bottom sutra box promotion record, etc. in ink Authority: For properties designated up to 2000, refer to "National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties, Special Volume," Comprehensive Catalog by Owner, Comprehensive Name Index, and Statistical Materials (Mainichi Shimbun, 2000). Changes since 2001 are noted separately. === Tangible Cultural Property designated by Kagawa Prefecture === *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Wooden lion head dedicated in the 5th year of Bun'an era Authority: {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|Q11667347|WD=Q11667347}} Site{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/kenkyoui/bunka/culturalassets.html |title=Cultural assets of Kagawa|publisher=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|accessdate=20209-01-05 }} === Access === About 10 minutes by car from the Shiratoriouchi Interchange on the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Expressway|Q7677132|WD=Q7677132}}. Located along {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Route 129 Suinushi Sanbonmatsu Line|Q11667426|WD=Q11667426|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Route 129}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === === Source === {{Reflist}} === (Tradition) Before the Nara period, the main shrine was rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} period (770-780) |Priest priest = Makichiyo Ouchi |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}Copper plate roof |Alternative name = Great Water Lord Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = Sanuki 15 shrines (No. 2) |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 18th|Q2521|WD=Q2521}} (Spring Regular Festival/Agricultural Tools Market) |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Mizunushi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} ``Mizushi Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454}}{{ill|lt=Higashikagawa City|Q861255|WD=Q861255}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wataridayi Hyakushohime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and the old {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Shakaku}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|lt=Sanukikuni|Q929682|WD=Q929682}}One of the 24 Shikinai shrines. === Enshrined God === ; Main enshrined deity * {{ill|lt=Watari-to-hi-momo-sou-hime-no-mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098|lt='''Watari-to-hi-momo-so-hime no mikoto'''}} ; precinct shrine * Korei Shrine - {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (Korei Tennou) Behind the main shrine, the father of the enshrined deity. * Kunidama Shrine - {{ill|lt=Wakoku Kahime|Q97539246|WD=Q97539246|lt=Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto}} (Yamato no Kunikahime no Mikoto) Right hand side of the main hall, mother of the enshrined deity. * Mizushu Sanzan (Kumano Sansha) Mountain worship It is said that Sojo Soun will be transferring the shrine. ** Shingu Shrine (Toramaruyama) - {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami Mikoto}} (Izanami Mikoto) ** Hongu Shrine (Mt. Hongu) - {{ill|lt=Hayatama no Omikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatama no Mikoto}} (Hayatama no Mikoto) ** Nachi Shrine (Mt. Nachi) - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=jikaiotokoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Kotosakao no Mikoto) * Water Shrine - {{jalink|Mizuhanome|Q8191796|Mizuhanome no Mikoto} (Mizuhanome no Mikoto) Also known as the Elephant Goddess or Seoritsuhime.* Daisui-dera Temple (founding date unknown) Suishinushi Shrine Bettoji Temple. The principal image is Amida Nyorai (to Enko-ji Temple), Fudo Myoo, Nidoji (to Yoda-ji Temple), and Eleven-faced Kannon (to Sakate/Kannon-ji Temple). Suitokusan Hojuin Jingu-ji Temple was changed to Daisui-dera Temple during the Kanbun year. '''{{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}''', Enreki 9th year (at the age of 24), visited Suishupu Shrine and Daisui-dera Temple. '''{{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}}''' dug the Aka well water in the precincts and dedicated it to the shrine (currently the well of Yegadani). Until the end of the Edo period, Shintoism and Buddhism were mixed, and in March of the first year of the Meiji era, an order was issued to separate Shintoism and Buddhism, and on February 25, 1887, the temple was officially closed down. === Overview === ; Outline It is located on the right bank of the Yoda River, at the foot of a mountain near Ouchi Dam. It is also called Omizusushi Daimyojin. The shrine bush behind the shrine is a natural environment conservation area for Kagawa Prefecture, and the Inori cedar within the precincts has been designated as a preserved tree by the prefecture. Many stone tools from the Jomon period and earthenware from the Yayoi and Kofun periods have been discovered in the vicinity, and there is also an ancient tomb on top of the mountain that is said to be the mausoleum of a princess, and the treasure trove contains many cultural assets. All of the shrine buildings are unified in {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|lt=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the standing stone marking the shrine's territory still remains in Ouchi and Shiratori-cho. According to the shrine's legend, the enshrined deity, Hyakushohime Wakatsubi, left the Kuroda Palace of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} when she was 8 years old, and was washed ashore here on a {{ill|lt=Tenban ship|Q126914107|WD=Q126914107|lt=Utsubo boat}}. Hyakushohime was excellent at predicting the future, and she made it rain for people suffering from sunlight, taught them about water sources, opened waterways, and helped them grow rice. There is a mausoleum on the mountain behind the shrine. ; Shrine{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=Mizunushi Shrine |publisher=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1227836/1/96 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}* {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa}}3rd year (836) November 1st Jinshin (7th) Article "Sanuki Province Water Lord Deity Ceremony Consecrated to Jugoi" {{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1194459/1/56 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}) * {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} On April 9, 866, Mijo Mijo, the water lord appears to be the ``Junior Fifth Rank Sewer Lord God of Jusanuki Province''{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/991094/1/105 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Japanese Three Dynasties True Story|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|lt=Three Generations True Story}}) * {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 3rd year (940): After praying for the pacification of the pirates, he was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi, and was further promoted to the rank of Shoichi ({{ill|lt=Chokan Kanbun|Q11651695|WD=Q11651695}}) * {{ill|lt=Koyasu|Q1327182|WD=Q1327182}}4th year (967) "Sanuki Province {{ill|lt=Ouchi District|Q31478|WD=Q31478}} Mizusushi Shrine" ({{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}})* At the time of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 (1872) {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, it was promoted to {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} * Promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} on January 23, 1903 * On March 22, 1907, designated as {{ill|lt=Shinsenheihakuryokushinsha|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinenheihakuryokushin Shrine}} ; History * The reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (around 290 BC|Q28200}}) Kuroda {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Andomiya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Watakutenhi Hyakusōhime no Mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098}} settled in Suishunushi at the age of 8 and was praised as the god of free water virtue. * {{ill|lt=Emperor Bunmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} 3rd year (699) {{ill|lt=Dainagon|Q1354706|WD=Q1354706}}{{ill|lt=Ishigamimaro|Q6086140|WD=Q6086140}} dedicates {{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} to our company and prays for the end of the epidemic * {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}}1st year to 12th year (770-780) Reconstruction of the Gotare ruins * {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}9 (790) Dengyo Daishi, {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}, at the age of 24, visited Suishinushi Shrine and Daisui Temple.* Around {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} (810-824), Kobo Daishi {{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}} dug a well of water in the grounds of Suishinushi Shrine and dedicated it to Suishinushi Shrine (it still exists in the grounds as the Well of Suishinushi). * {{ill|lt=Tenryaku|Q1346281|WD=Q1346281}} 2nd year (948) {{ill|lt=Doctor of Literature|Q11500074|WD=Q11500074}} Tachibana Naoki goes as {{ill|lt=Imperial Envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920}} * {{ill|lt=Owa|Q1324768|WD=Q1324768}}2nd year (962){{ill|lt=Council|Q1133663|WD=Q1133663}}{{ill|lt=Tadasuke Fujiwara|Q11623 263|WD=Q11623 263|lt=Fujiwara Asaomi Tadasuke}} worshiped the {{ill|lt=Imperial edict|Q905765|WD=Q905765}} and prayed for rain, and there was a proclamation, and the manor was called Ōsuinushi, and the name was called Ōsuinushi Taisha. * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}} 1st year (1131) Visit of Sanuki {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Tsunetaka Fujiwara|Q134963520|WD=Q134963520}}* {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}}2 years (1132) {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} which was 2 towns, 2 tans and 60 feet became 24 towns and 5 tans. Around this time, there are records that {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Mokudai|Q11581544|WD=Q11581544}} frequently visited the shrine. It is believed that {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} was donated in the form of dividing a part of Yoda-go and Irino-go, which were Garyo|Q11421344}}. {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367}} It reached the peak of prosperity with 75 members and 42 {{ill|lt=Sōbo|Q11387615|WD=Q11387615|lt=Sōbo}}. assets === === Important cultural property === *7 wooden statues of godsThe history of this item's designation as a cultural property is as follows. *In 1901 (Meiji 34), the following three buildings (total of eight buildings) were designated as important cultural properties (formerly national treasures). (Ministry of Home Affairs Notification No. 20, March 27, 1909) **Three wooden statues of the gods (Seated statue of Yamatobi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto, Seated statue of Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto, and Seated statue of Owa Nekohikotaikion no Mikoto) **1 wooden seated male god statue **4 wooden seated goddess statues *Of the ``Three Wooden God Statues'', the Watatsubi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto statue was purchased by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2017 after its whereabouts were unknown for a while. *In 2020 (Reiwa 2), the ``Three Wooden God Statues'' were divided into two: ``Seated Statue of Watanbi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto'' (Kept by the Agency for Cultural Affairs) and ``Seated Statue of Wakoku Kaohime no Mikoto/Owa Nekohiko Taisho Hime no Mikoto'' (Owned by Mizunushi Shrine). The latter was combined with two previously designated Important Cultural Properties (``1 Wooden Seated Statue of a Male God'' and ``4 Wooden Seated Statues of a Goddess'') and was designated as an Important Cultural Property under the name ``7 Wooden God Statues''. (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 120, September 30, 2020) *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Raimon Radenkura *{{ill|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra|Q15905785|WD=Q15905785|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra}} 600 chapters included: 60 sutra boxes, inner bottom sutra box promotion record, etc. in ink Authority: For properties designated up to 2000, refer to "National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties, Special Volume," Comprehensive Catalog by Owner, Comprehensive Name Index, and Statistical Materials (Mainichi Shimbun, 2000). Changes since 2001 are noted separately. === Tangible Cultural Property designated by Kagawa Prefecture === *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Wooden lion head dedicated in the 5th year of Bun'an era Authority: {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|Q11667347|WD=Q11667347}} Site{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/kenkyoui/bunka/culturalassets.html |title=Cultural assets of Kagawa|publisher=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|accessdate=20209-01-05 }} === Access === About 10 minutes by car from the Shiratoriouchi Interchange on the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Expressway|Q7677132|WD=Q7677132}}. Main enshrined deity * {{ill|lt=Watari-to-hi-momo-sou-hime-no-mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098|lt='''Watari-to-hi-momo-so-hime no mikoto'''}} ; precinct shrine * Korei Shrine - {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (Korei Tennou) Behind the main shrine, the father of the enshrined deity. * Kunidama Shrine - {{ill|lt=Wakoku Kahime|Q97539246|WD=Q97539246|lt=Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto}} (Yamato no Kunikahime no Mikoto) Right hand side of the main hall, mother of the enshrined deity. * Mizushu Sanzan (Kumano Sansha) Mountain worship It is said that Sojo Soun will be transferring the shrine. ** Shingu Shrine (Toramaruyama) - {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami Mikoto}} (Izanami Mikoto) ** Hongu Shrine (Mt. Hongu) - {{ill|lt=Hayatama no Omikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatama no Mikoto}} (Hayatama no Mikoto) ** Nachi Shrine (Mt. Nachi) - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=jikaiotokoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Kotosakao no Mikoto) * Water Shrine - {{jalink|Mizuhanome|Q8191796|Mizuhanome no Mikoto} (Mizuhanome no Mikoto) Also known as the Elephant Goddess or Seoritsuhime.* Daisui-dera Temple (founding date unknown) Suishinushi Shrine Bettoji Temple. The principal image is Amida Nyorai (to Enko-ji Temple), Fudo Myoo, Nidoji (to Yoda-ji Temple), and Eleven-faced Kannon (to Sakate/Kannon-ji Temple). Suitokusan Hojuin Jingu-ji Temple was changed to Daisui-dera Temple during the Kanbun year. '''{{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}''', Enreki 9th year (at the age of 24), visited Suishupu Shrine and Daisui-dera Temple. '''{{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}}''' dug the Aka well water in the precincts and dedicated it to the shrine (currently the well of Yegadani). Until the end of the Edo period, Shintoism and Buddhism were mixed, and in March of the first year of the Meiji era, an order was issued to separate Shintoism and Buddhism, and on February 25, 1887, the temple was officially closed down. === Overview === ; Outline It is located on the right bank of the Yoda River, at the foot of a mountain near Ouchi Dam. It is also called Omizusushi Daimyojin. The shrine bush behind the shrine is a natural environment conservation area for Kagawa Prefecture, and the Inori cedar within the precincts has been designated as a preserved tree by the prefecture. Many stone tools from the Jomon period and earthenware from the Yayoi and Kofun periods have been discovered in the vicinity, and there is also an ancient tomb on top of the mountain that is said to be the mausoleum of a princess, and the treasure trove contains many cultural assets. All of the shrine buildings are unified in {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|lt=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the standing stone marking the shrine's territory still remains in Ouchi and Shiratori-cho. According to the shrine's legend, the enshrined deity, Hyakushohime Wakatsubi, left the Kuroda Palace of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} when she was 8 years old, and was washed ashore here on a {{ill|lt=Tenban ship|Q126914107|WD=Q126914107|lt=Utsubo boat}}. Hyakushohime was excellent at predicting the future, and she made it rain for people suffering from sunlight, taught them about water sources, opened waterways, and helped them grow rice. There is a mausoleum on the mountain behind the shrine. ; Shrine{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=Mizunushi Shrine |publisher=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1227836/1/96 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}* {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa}}3rd year (836) November 1st Jinshin (7th) Article "Sanuki Province Water Lord Deity Ceremony Consecrated to Jugoi" {{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1194459/1/56 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}) * {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} On April 9, 866, Mijo Mijo, the water lord appears to be the ``Junior Fifth Rank Sewer Lord God of Jusanuki Province''{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/991094/1/105 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Japanese Three Dynasties True Story|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|lt=Three Generations True Story}}) * {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 3rd year (940): After praying for the pacification of the pirates, he was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi, and was further promoted to the rank of Shoichi ({{ill|lt=Chokan Kanbun|Q11651695|WD=Q11651695}}) * {{ill|lt=Koyasu|Q1327182|WD=Q1327182}}4th year (967) "Sanuki Province {{ill|lt=Ouchi District|Q31478|WD=Q31478}} Mizusushi Shrine" ({{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}})* At the time of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 (1872) {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, it was promoted to {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} * Promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} on January 23, 1903 * On March 22, 1907, designated as {{ill|lt=Shinsenheihakuryokushinsha|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinenheihakuryokushin Shrine}} ; History * The reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (around 290 BC|Q28200}}) Kuroda {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Andomiya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Watakutenhi Hyakusōhime no Mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098}} settled in Suishunushi at the age of 8 and was praised as the god of free water virtue. * {{ill|lt=Emperor Bunmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} 3rd year (699) {{ill|lt=Dainagon|Q1354706|WD=Q1354706}}{{ill|lt=Ishigamimaro|Q6086140|WD=Q6086140}} dedicates {{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} to our company and prays for the end of the epidemic * {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}}1st year to 12th year (770-780) Reconstruction of the Gotare ruins * {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}9 (790) Dengyo Daishi, {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}, at the age of 24, visited Suishinushi Shrine and Daisui Temple.* Around {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} (810-824), Kobo Daishi {{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}} dug a well of water in the grounds of Suishinushi Shrine and dedicated it to Suishinushi Shrine (it still exists in the grounds as the Well of Suishinushi). * {{ill|lt=Tenryaku|Q1346281|WD=Q1346281}} 2nd year (948) {{ill|lt=Doctor of Literature|Q11500074|WD=Q11500074}} Tachibana Naoki goes as {{ill|lt=Imperial Envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920}} * {{ill|lt=Owa|Q1324768|WD=Q1324768}}2nd year (962){{ill|lt=Council|Q1133663|WD=Q1133663}}{{ill|lt=Tadasuke Fujiwara|Q11623 263|WD=Q11623 263|lt=Fujiwara Asaomi Tadasuke}} worshiped the {{ill|lt=Imperial edict|Q905765|WD=Q905765}} and prayed for rain, and there was a proclamation, and the manor was called Ōsuinushi, and the name was called Ōsuinushi Taisha. * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}} 1st year (1131) Visit of Sanuki {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Tsunetaka Fujiwara|Q134963520|WD=Q134963520}}* {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}}2 years (1132) {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} which was 2 towns, 2 tans and 60 feet became 24 towns and 5 tans. Around this time, there are records that {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Mokudai|Q11581544|WD=Q11581544}} frequently visited the shrine. It is believed that {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} was donated in the form of dividing a part of Yoda-go and Irino-go, which were Garyo|Q11421344}}. {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367}} It reached the peak of prosperity with 75 members and 42 {{ill|lt=Sōbo|Q11387615|WD=Q11387615|lt=Sōbo}}. assets === === Important cultural property === *7 wooden statues of godsThe history of this item's designation as a cultural property is as follows. *In 1901 (Meiji 34), the following three buildings (total of eight buildings) were designated as important cultural properties (formerly national treasures). (Ministry of Home Affairs Notification No. 20, March 27, 1909) **Three wooden statues of the gods (Seated statue of Yamatobi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto, Seated statue of Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto, and Seated statue of Owa Nekohikotaikion no Mikoto) **1 wooden seated male god statue **4 wooden seated goddess statues *Of the ``Three Wooden God Statues'', the Watatsubi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto statue was purchased by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2017 after its whereabouts were unknown for a while. *In 2020 (Reiwa 2), the ``Three Wooden God Statues'' were divided into two: ``Seated Statue of Watanbi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto'' (Kept by the Agency for Cultural Affairs) and ``Seated Statue of Wakoku Kaohime no Mikoto/Owa Nekohiko Taisho Hime no Mikoto'' (Owned by Mizunushi Shrine). The latter was combined with two previously designated Important Cultural Properties (``1 Wooden Seated Statue of a Male God'' and ``4 Wooden Seated Statues of a Goddess'') and was designated as an Important Cultural Property under the name ``7 Wooden God Statues''. (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 120, September 30, 2020) *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Raimon Radenkura *{{ill|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra|Q15905785|WD=Q15905785|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra}} 600 chapters included: 60 sutra boxes, inner bottom sutra box promotion record, etc. in ink Authority: For properties designated up to 2000, refer to "National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties, Special Volume," Comprehensive Catalog by Owner, Comprehensive Name Index, and Statistical Materials (Mainichi Shimbun, 2000). Changes since 2001 are noted separately. === Tangible Cultural Property designated by Kagawa Prefecture === *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Wooden lion head dedicated in the 5th year of Bun'an era Authority: {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|Q11667347|WD=Q11667347}} Site{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/kenkyoui/bunka/culturalassets.html |title=Cultural assets of Kagawa|publisher=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|accessdate=20209-01-05 }} === Access === About 10 minutes by car from the Shiratoriouchi Interchange on the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Expressway|Q7677132|WD=Q7677132}}. Oratory |Location = {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454}}{{ill|lt=Higashikagawa City|Q861255|WD=Q861255}}Suishinushi 1418-1 |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Watariday Himozuhime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shintai = Mausoleum |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small)

(Tradition) Before the Nara period, the main shrine was rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} period (770-780) |Priest priest = Makichiyo Ouchi |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}Copper plate roof |Alternative name = Great Water Lord Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = Sanuki 15 shrines (No. 2) |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 18th|Q2521|WD=Q2521}} (Spring Regular Festival/Agricultural Tools Market) |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Mizunushi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} ``Mizushi Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454}}{{ill|lt=Higashikagawa City|Q861255|WD=Q861255}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wataridayi Hyakushohime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and the old {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Shakaku}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. {{ill|lt=Sanukikuni|Q929682|WD=Q929682}}One of the 24 Shikinai shrines. === Enshrined God === ; Main enshrined deity * {{ill|lt=Watari-to-hi-momo-sou-hime-no-mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098|lt='''Watari-to-hi-momo-so-hime no mikoto'''}} ; precinct shrine * Korei Shrine - {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (Korei Tennou) Behind the main shrine, the father of the enshrined deity. * Kunidama Shrine - {{ill|lt=Wakoku Kahime|Q97539246|WD=Q97539246|lt=Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto}} (Yamato no Kunikahime no Mikoto) Right hand side of the main hall, mother of the enshrined deity. * Mizushu Sanzan (Kumano Sansha) Mountain worship It is said that Sojo Soun will be transferring the shrine. ** Shingu Shrine (Toramaruyama) - {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami Mikoto}} (Izanami Mikoto) ** Hongu Shrine (Mt. Hongu) - {{ill|lt=Hayatama no Omikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatama no Mikoto}} (Hayatama no Mikoto) ** Nachi Shrine (Mt. Nachi) - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=jikaiotokoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Kotosakao no Mikoto) * Water Shrine - {{jalink|Mizuhanome|Q8191796|Mizuhanome no Mikoto} (Mizuhanome no Mikoto) Also known as the Elephant Goddess or Seoritsuhime.* Daisui-dera Temple (founding date unknown) Suishinushi Shrine Bettoji Temple. The principal image is Amida Nyorai (to Enko-ji Temple), Fudo Myoo, Nidoji (to Yoda-ji Temple), and Eleven-faced Kannon (to Sakate/Kannon-ji Temple). Suitokusan Hojuin Jingu-ji Temple was changed to Daisui-dera Temple during the Kanbun year. '''{{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}''', Enreki 9th year (at the age of 24), visited Suishupu Shrine and Daisui-dera Temple. '''{{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}}''' dug the Aka well water in the precincts and dedicated it to the shrine (currently the well of Yegadani). Until the end of the Edo period, Shintoism and Buddhism were mixed, and in March of the first year of the Meiji era, an order was issued to separate Shintoism and Buddhism, and on February 25, 1887, the temple was officially closed down. === Overview === ; Outline It is located on the right bank of the Yoda River, at the foot of a mountain near Ouchi Dam. It is also called Omizusushi Daimyojin. The shrine bush behind the shrine is a natural environment conservation area for Kagawa Prefecture, and the Inori cedar within the precincts has been designated as a preserved tree by the prefecture. Many stone tools from the Jomon period and earthenware from the Yayoi and Kofun periods have been discovered in the vicinity, and there is also an ancient tomb on top of the mountain that is said to be the mausoleum of a princess, and the treasure trove contains many cultural assets. All of the shrine buildings are unified in {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|lt=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the standing stone marking the shrine's territory still remains in Ouchi and Shiratori-cho. According to the shrine's legend, the enshrined deity, Hyakushohime Wakatsubi, left the Kuroda Palace of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} when she was 8 years old, and was washed ashore here on a {{ill|lt=Tenban ship|Q126914107|WD=Q126914107|lt=Utsubo boat}}. Hyakushohime was excellent at predicting the future, and she made it rain for people suffering from sunlight, taught them about water sources, opened waterways, and helped them grow rice. There is a mausoleum on the mountain behind the shrine. ; Shrine{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=Mizunushi Shrine |publisher=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1227836/1/96 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}* {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa}}3rd year (836) November 1st Jinshin (7th) Article "Sanuki Province Water Lord Deity Ceremony Consecrated to Jugoi" {{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1194459/1/56 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}) * {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} On April 9, 866, Mijo Mijo, the water lord appears to be the ``Junior Fifth Rank Sewer Lord God of Jusanuki Province''{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/991094/1/105 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Japanese Three Dynasties True Story|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|lt=Three Generations True Story}}) * {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 3rd year (940): After praying for the pacification of the pirates, he was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi, and was further promoted to the rank of Shoichi ({{ill|lt=Chokan Kanbun|Q11651695|WD=Q11651695}}) * {{ill|lt=Koyasu|Q1327182|WD=Q1327182}}4th year (967) "Sanuki Province {{ill|lt=Ouchi District|Q31478|WD=Q31478}} Mizusushi Shrine" ({{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}})* At the time of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 (1872) {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, it was promoted to {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} * Promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} on January 23, 1903 * On March 22, 1907, designated as {{ill|lt=Shinsenheihakuryokushinsha|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinenheihakuryokushin Shrine}} ; History * The reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (around 290 BC|Q28200}}) Kuroda {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Andomiya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Watakutenhi Hyakusōhime no Mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098}} settled in Suishunushi at the age of 8 and was praised as the god of free water virtue. * {{ill|lt=Emperor Bunmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} 3rd year (699) {{ill|lt=Dainagon|Q1354706|WD=Q1354706}}{{ill|lt=Ishigamimaro|Q6086140|WD=Q6086140}} dedicates {{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} to our company and prays for the end of the epidemic * {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}}1st year to 12th year (770-780) Reconstruction of the Gotare ruins * {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}9 (790) Dengyo Daishi, {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}, at the age of 24, visited Suishinushi Shrine and Daisui Temple.* Around {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} (810-824), Kobo Daishi {{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}} dug a well of water in the grounds of Suishinushi Shrine and dedicated it to Suishinushi Shrine (it still exists in the grounds as the Well of Suishinushi). * {{ill|lt=Tenryaku|Q1346281|WD=Q1346281}} 2nd year (948) {{ill|lt=Doctor of Literature|Q11500074|WD=Q11500074}} Tachibana Naoki goes as {{ill|lt=Imperial Envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920}} * {{ill|lt=Owa|Q1324768|WD=Q1324768}}2nd year (962){{ill|lt=Council|Q1133663|WD=Q1133663}}{{ill|lt=Tadasuke Fujiwara|Q11623 263|WD=Q11623 263|lt=Fujiwara Asaomi Tadasuke}} worshiped the {{ill|lt=Imperial edict|Q905765|WD=Q905765}} and prayed for rain, and there was a proclamation, and the manor was called Ōsuinushi, and the name was called Ōsuinushi Taisha. * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}} 1st year (1131) Visit of Sanuki {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Tsunetaka Fujiwara|Q134963520|WD=Q134963520}}* {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}}2 years (1132) {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} which was 2 towns, 2 tans and 60 feet became 24 towns and 5 tans. Around this time, there are records that {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Mokudai|Q11581544|WD=Q11581544}} frequently visited the shrine. It is believed that {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} was donated in the form of dividing a part of Yoda-go and Irino-go, which were Garyo|Q11421344}}. {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367}} It reached the peak of prosperity with 75 members and 42 {{ill|lt=Sōbo|Q11387615|WD=Q11387615|lt=Sōbo}}. (Mizunushi Shrine Great Pannya Sutra Box Book) * {{ill|lt=Chojo|Q1194264|WD=Q1194264}}2nd year (1133) {{ill|lt=Mokudai|Q11581544|WD=Q11581544}}Visit of Morimichi Kawachimae * {{ill|lt=Chohiro|Q1193904|WD=Q1193904}}Visit of Sanuki Kokushi {{ill|lt=Hideyoshi Fujiwara|Q11623440|WD=Q11623440}} in 1163 * {{ill|lt=Jin'an (Japan)|Q3577285|WD=Q3577285|lt=Jin'an}}2nd year (1167){{ill|lt=Mokudai|Q11581544|WD=Q11581544}}{{ill|lt=Kokiyo Tachibana|Q121355828|WD=Q121355828}} visit * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}15 years (1587) {{ill|lt=Ikoma Chikamasa|Q1059845|WD=Q1059845|lt=Ikoma Kinnori}} He was highly revered, and the fourth generation {{ill|lt=Takatoshi Ikoma|Q5996119|WD=Q5996119|lt=Takatoshi}} repaired the shrine and donated 35 koku of shrine territory (Ikoma Chronicles).* {{ill|lt=Masakazu|Q1324807|WD=Q1324807}} 1st year (1681) {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915}} Lord {{ill|lt=Yorishige Matsudaira|Q3299364|WD=Q3299364}} worship * {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 6th year (1693) {{ill|lt=Yoritsune Matsudaira|Q11530633|WD=Q11530633}} visited the shrine, repaired the shrine building, and donated 35 koku of rice fields. * {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}}The current main shrine was rebuilt in August 1858. * {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}The shrine was rebuilt in June 1865. * At the time of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 (1872) {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, it was promoted to {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} * Promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} on January 23, 1903 * On March 22, 1907, designated as {{ill|lt=Shinsenheihakuryokushinsha|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinenheihakuryokushin Shrine}} === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property === *7 wooden statues of godsThe history of this item's designation as a cultural property is as follows. *In 1901 (Meiji 34), the following three buildings (total of eight buildings) were designated as important cultural properties (formerly national treasures). (Ministry of Home Affairs Notification No. 20, March 27, 1909) **Three wooden statues of the gods (Seated statue of Yamatobi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto, Seated statue of Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto, and Seated statue of Owa Nekohikotaikion no Mikoto) **1 wooden seated male god statue **4 wooden seated goddess statues *Of the ``Three Wooden God Statues'', the Watatsubi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto statue was purchased by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2017 after its whereabouts were unknown for a while. *In 2020 (Reiwa 2), the ``Three Wooden God Statues'' were divided into two: ``Seated Statue of Watanbi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto'' (Kept by the Agency for Cultural Affairs) and ``Seated Statue of Wakoku Kaohime no Mikoto/Owa Nekohiko Taisho Hime no Mikoto'' (Owned by Mizunushi Shrine). The latter was combined with two previously designated Important Cultural Properties (``1 Wooden Seated Statue of a Male God'' and ``4 Wooden Seated Statues of a Goddess'') and was designated as an Important Cultural Property under the name ``7 Wooden God Statues''. (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 120, September 30, 2020) *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Raimon Radenkura *{{ill|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra|Q15905785|WD=Q15905785|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra}} 600 chapters included: 60 sutra boxes, inner bottom sutra box promotion record, etc. in ink Authority: For properties designated up to 2000, refer to "National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties, Special Volume," Comprehensive Catalog by Owner, Comprehensive Name Index, and Statistical Materials (Mainichi Shimbun, 2000). Changes since 2001 are noted separately. === Tangible Cultural Property designated by Kagawa Prefecture === *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Wooden lion head dedicated in the 5th year of Bun'an era Authority: {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|Q11667347|WD=Q11667347}} Site{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/kenkyoui/bunka/culturalassets.html |title=Cultural assets of Kagawa|publisher=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|accessdate=20209-01-05 }} === Access === About 10 minutes by car from the Shiratoriouchi Interchange on the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Expressway|Q7677132|WD=Q7677132}}. Located along {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Route 129 Suinushi Sanbonmatsu Line|Q11667426|WD=Q11667426|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Route 129}}. === Footnotes === === Notes === === Source === {{Reflist}} === (Tradition) Before the Nara period, the main shrine was rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} period (770-780) |Priest priest = Makichiyo Ouchi |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}Copper plate roof |Alternative name = Great Water Lord Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = Sanuki 15 shrines (No. 2) |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 18th|Q2521|WD=Q2521}} (Spring Regular Festival/Agricultural Tools Market) |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Mizunushi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} ``Mizushi Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454}}{{ill|lt=Higashikagawa City|Q861255|WD=Q861255}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wataridayi Hyakushohime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and the old {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Shakaku}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. {{ill|lt=Sanukikuni|Q929682|WD=Q929682}}One of the 24 Shikinai shrines. === Enshrined God === ; Main enshrined deity * {{ill|lt=Watari-to-hi-momo-sou-hime-no-mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098|lt='''Watari-to-hi-momo-so-hime no mikoto'''}} ; precinct shrine * Korei Shrine - {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (Korei Tennou) Behind the main shrine, the father of the enshrined deity. * Kunidama Shrine - {{ill|lt=Wakoku Kahime|Q97539246|WD=Q97539246|lt=Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto}} (Yamato no Kunikahime no Mikoto) Right hand side of the main hall, mother of the enshrined deity. * Mizushu Sanzan (Kumano Sansha) Mountain worship It is said that Sojo Soun will be transferring the shrine. ** Shingu Shrine (Toramaruyama) - {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami Mikoto}} (Izanami Mikoto) ** Hongu Shrine (Mt. Hongu) - {{ill|lt=Hayatama no Omikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatama no Mikoto}} (Hayatama no Mikoto) ** Nachi Shrine (Mt. Nachi) - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=jikaiotokoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Kotosakao no Mikoto) * Water Shrine - {{jalink|Mizuhanome|Q8191796|Mizuhanome no Mikoto} (Mizuhanome no Mikoto) Also known as the Elephant Goddess or Seoritsuhime.* Daisui-dera Temple (founding date unknown) Suishinushi Shrine Bettoji Temple. The principal image is Amida Nyorai (to Enko-ji Temple), Fudo Myoo, Nidoji (to Yoda-ji Temple), and Eleven-faced Kannon (to Sakate/Kannon-ji Temple). Suitokusan Hojuin Jingu-ji Temple was changed to Daisui-dera Temple during the Kanbun year. '''{{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}''', Enreki 9th year (at the age of 24), visited Suishupu Shrine and Daisui-dera Temple. '''{{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}}''' dug the Aka well water in the precincts and dedicated it to the shrine (currently the well of Yegadani). Until the end of the Edo period, Shintoism and Buddhism were mixed, and in March of the first year of the Meiji era, an order was issued to separate Shintoism and Buddhism, and on February 25, 1887, the temple was officially closed down. === Overview === ; Outline It is located on the right bank of the Yoda River, at the foot of a mountain near Ouchi Dam. It is also called Omizusushi Daimyojin. The shrine bush behind the shrine is a natural environment conservation area for Kagawa Prefecture, and the Inori cedar within the precincts has been designated as a preserved tree by the prefecture. Many stone tools from the Jomon period and earthenware from the Yayoi and Kofun periods have been discovered in the vicinity, and there is also an ancient tomb on top of the mountain that is said to be the mausoleum of a princess, and the treasure trove contains many cultural assets. All of the shrine buildings are unified in {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|lt=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the standing stone marking the shrine's territory still remains in Ouchi and Shiratori-cho. According to the shrine's legend, the enshrined deity, Hyakushohime Wakatsubi, left the Kuroda Palace of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} when she was 8 years old, and was washed ashore here on a {{ill|lt=Tenban ship|Q126914107|WD=Q126914107|lt=Utsubo boat}}. Hyakushohime was excellent at predicting the future, and she made it rain for people suffering from sunlight, taught them about water sources, opened waterways, and helped them grow rice. There is a mausoleum on the mountain behind the shrine. ; Shrine{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=Mizunushi Shrine |publisher=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1227836/1/96 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}* {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa}}3rd year (836) November 1st Jinshin (7th) Article "Sanuki Province Water Lord Deity Ceremony Consecrated to Jugoi" {{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1194459/1/56 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}) * {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} On April 9, 866, Mijo Mijo, the water lord appears to be the ``Junior Fifth Rank Sewer Lord God of Jusanuki Province''{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/991094/1/105 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Japanese Three Dynasties True Story|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|lt=Three Generations True Story}}) * {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 3rd year (940): After praying for the pacification of the pirates, he was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi, and was further promoted to the rank of Shoichi ({{ill|lt=Chokan Kanbun|Q11651695|WD=Q11651695}}) * {{ill|lt=Koyasu|Q1327182|WD=Q1327182}}4th year (967) "Sanuki Province {{ill|lt=Ouchi District|Q31478|WD=Q31478}} Mizusushi Shrine" ({{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}})* At the time of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 (1872) {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, it was promoted to {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} * Promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} on January 23, 1903 * On March 22, 1907, designated as {{ill|lt=Shinsenheihakuryokushinsha|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinenheihakuryokushin Shrine}} ; History * The reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (around 290 BC|Q28200}}) Kuroda {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Andomiya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Watakutenhi Hyakusōhime no Mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098}} settled in Suishunushi at the age of 8 and was praised as the god of free water virtue. * {{ill|lt=Emperor Bunmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} 3rd year (699) {{ill|lt=Dainagon|Q1354706|WD=Q1354706}}{{ill|lt=Ishigamimaro|Q6086140|WD=Q6086140}} dedicates {{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} to our company and prays for the end of the epidemic * {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}}1st year to 12th year (770-780) Reconstruction of the Gotare ruins * {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}9 (790) Dengyo Daishi, {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}, at the age of 24, visited Suishinushi Shrine and Daisui Temple.* Around {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} (810-824), Kobo Daishi {{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}} dug a well of water in the grounds of Suishinushi Shrine and dedicated it to Suishinushi Shrine (it still exists in the grounds as the Well of Suishinushi). * {{ill|lt=Tenryaku|Q1346281|WD=Q1346281}} 2nd year (948) {{ill|lt=Doctor of Literature|Q11500074|WD=Q11500074}} Tachibana Naoki goes as {{ill|lt=Imperial Envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920}} * {{ill|lt=Owa|Q1324768|WD=Q1324768}}2nd year (962){{ill|lt=Council|Q1133663|WD=Q1133663}}{{ill|lt=Tadasuke Fujiwara|Q11623 263|WD=Q11623 263|lt=Fujiwara Asaomi Tadasuke}} worshiped the {{ill|lt=Imperial edict|Q905765|WD=Q905765}} and prayed for rain, and there was a proclamation, and the manor was called Ōsuinushi, and the name was called Ōsuinushi Taisha. * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}} 1st year (1131) Visit of Sanuki {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Tsunetaka Fujiwara|Q134963520|WD=Q134963520}}* {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}}2 years (1132) {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} which was 2 towns, 2 tans and 60 feet became 24 towns and 5 tans. Around this time, there are records that {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Mokudai|Q11581544|WD=Q11581544}} frequently visited the shrine. It is believed that {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} was donated in the form of dividing a part of Yoda-go and Irino-go, which were Garyo|Q11421344}}. {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367}} It reached the peak of prosperity with 75 members and 42 {{ill|lt=Sōbo|Q11387615|WD=Q11387615|lt=Sōbo}}. assets === === Important cultural property === *7 wooden statues of godsThe history of this item's designation as a cultural property is as follows. *In 1901 (Meiji 34), the following three buildings (total of eight buildings) were designated as important cultural properties (formerly national treasures). (Ministry of Home Affairs Notification No. 20, March 27, 1909) **Three wooden statues of the gods (Seated statue of Yamatobi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto, Seated statue of Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto, and Seated statue of Owa Nekohikotaikion no Mikoto) **1 wooden seated male god statue **4 wooden seated goddess statues *Of the ``Three Wooden God Statues'', the Watatsubi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto statue was purchased by the Agency for Cultural Affairs in 2017 after its whereabouts were unknown for a while. *In 2020 (Reiwa 2), the ``Three Wooden God Statues'' were divided into two: ``Seated Statue of Watanbi Hyakusho Hime no Mikoto'' (Kept by the Agency for Cultural Affairs) and ``Seated Statue of Wakoku Kaohime no Mikoto/Owa Nekohiko Taisho Hime no Mikoto'' (Owned by Mizunushi Shrine). The latter was combined with two previously designated Important Cultural Properties (``1 Wooden Seated Statue of a Male God'' and ``4 Wooden Seated Statues of a Goddess'') and was designated as an Important Cultural Property under the name ``7 Wooden God Statues''. (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 120, September 30, 2020) *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Raimon Radenkura *{{ill|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra|Q15905785|WD=Q15905785|lt=Great Prajnaparamita Sutra}} 600 chapters included: 60 sutra boxes, inner bottom sutra box promotion record, etc. in ink Authority: For properties designated up to 2000, refer to "National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties, Special Volume," Comprehensive Catalog by Owner, Comprehensive Name Index, and Statistical Materials (Mainichi Shimbun, 2000). Changes since 2001 are noted separately. === Tangible Cultural Property designated by Kagawa Prefecture === *Pair of wooden guardian dogs *Wooden lion head dedicated in the 5th year of Bun'an era Authority: {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|Q11667347|WD=Q11667347}} Site{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://www.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/kenkyoui/bunka/culturalassets.html |title=Cultural assets of Kagawa|publisher=Kagawa Prefectural Board of Education|accessdate=20209-01-05 }} === Access === About 10 minutes by car from the Shiratoriouchi Interchange on the {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Expressway|Q7677132|WD=Q7677132}}. (Tradition) Before the Nara period, the main shrine was rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} period (770-780) |Priest priest = Makichiyo Ouchi |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}Copper plate roof |Alternative name = Great Water Lord Daimyojin |Fudasho, etc. = Sanuki 15 shrines (No. 2) |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 18th|Q2521|WD=Q2521}} (Spring Regular Festival/Agricultural Tools Market) |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Mizunushi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} ``Mizushi Shrine'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454}}{{ill|lt=Higashikagawa City|Q861255|WD=Q861255}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Wataridayi Hyakushohime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} is enshrined here. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and the old {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Shakaku}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. {{ill|lt=Sanukikuni|Q929682|WD=Q929682}}One of the 24 Shikinai shrines. === Enshrined God === ; Main enshrined deity * {{ill|lt=Watari-to-hi-momo-sou-hime-no-mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098|lt='''Watari-to-hi-momo-so-hime no mikoto'''}} ; precinct shrine * Korei Shrine - {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (Korei Tennou) Behind the main shrine, the father of the enshrined deity. * Kunidama Shrine - {{ill|lt=Wakoku Kahime|Q97539246|WD=Q97539246|lt=Wakoku Kahime no Mikoto}} (Yamato no Kunikahime no Mikoto) Right hand side of the main hall, mother of the enshrined deity. * Mizushu Sanzan (Kumano Sansha) Mountain worship It is said that Sojo Soun will be transferring the shrine. ** Shingu Shrine (Toramaruyama) - {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami Mikoto}} (Izanami Mikoto) ** Hongu Shrine (Mt. Hongu) - {{ill|lt=Hayatama no Omikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayatama no Mikoto}} (Hayatama no Mikoto) ** Nachi Shrine (Mt. Nachi) - {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=jikaiotokoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Kotosakao no Mikoto) * Water Shrine - {{jalink|Mizuhanome|Q8191796|Mizuhanome no Mikoto} (Mizuhanome no Mikoto) Also known as the Elephant Goddess or Seoritsuhime.* Daisui-dera Temple (founding date unknown) Suishinushi Shrine Bettoji Temple. The principal image is Amida Nyorai (to Enko-ji Temple), Fudo Myoo, Nidoji (to Yoda-ji Temple), and Eleven-faced Kannon (to Sakate/Kannon-ji Temple). Suitokusan Hojuin Jingu-ji Temple was changed to Daisui-dera Temple during the Kanbun year. '''{{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}''', Enreki 9th year (at the age of 24), visited Suishupu Shrine and Daisui-dera Temple. '''{{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}}''' dug the Aka well water in the precincts and dedicated it to the shrine (currently the well of Yegadani). Until the end of the Edo period, Shintoism and Buddhism were mixed, and in March of the first year of the Meiji era, an order was issued to separate Shintoism and Buddhism, and on February 25, 1887, the temple was officially closed down. === Overview === ; Outline It is located on the right bank of the Yoda River, at the foot of a mountain near Ouchi Dam. It is also called Omizusushi Daimyojin. The shrine bush behind the shrine is a natural environment conservation area for Kagawa Prefecture, and the Inori cedar within the precincts has been designated as a preserved tree by the prefecture. Many stone tools from the Jomon period and earthenware from the Yayoi and Kofun periods have been discovered in the vicinity, and there is also an ancient tomb on top of the mountain that is said to be the mausoleum of a princess, and the treasure trove contains many cultural assets. All of the shrine buildings are unified in {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|lt=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the standing stone marking the shrine's territory still remains in Ouchi and Shiratori-cho. According to the shrine's legend, the enshrined deity, Hyakushohime Wakatsubi, left the Kuroda Palace of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} when she was 8 years old, and was washed ashore here on a {{ill|lt=Tenban ship|Q126914107|WD=Q126914107|lt=Utsubo boat}}. Hyakushohime was excellent at predicting the future, and she made it rain for people suffering from sunlight, taught them about water sources, opened waterways, and helped them grow rice. There is a mausoleum on the mountain behind the shrine. ; Shrine{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=Mizunushi Shrine |publisher=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1227836/1/96 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}* {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa}}3rd year (836) November 1st Jinshin (7th) Article "Sanuki Province Water Lord Deity Ceremony Consecrated to Jugoi" {{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1194459/1/56 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}) * {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} On April 9, 866, Mijo Mijo, the water lord appears to be the ``Junior Fifth Rank Sewer Lord God of Jusanuki Province''{{Cite web|Japanese book|title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/991094/1/105 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2023-06-15}}({{ill|lt=Japanese Three Dynasties True Story|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|lt=Three Generations True Story}}) * {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340}} 3rd year (940): After praying for the pacification of the pirates, he was promoted to the lower rank of Shogoi, and was further promoted to the rank of Shoichi ({{ill|lt=Chokan Kanbun|Q11651695|WD=Q11651695}}) * {{ill|lt=Koyasu|Q1327182|WD=Q1327182}}4th year (967) "Sanuki Province {{ill|lt=Ouchi District|Q31478|WD=Q31478}} Mizusushi Shrine" ({{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmecho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}})* At the time of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 (1872) {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, it was promoted to {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} * Promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} on January 23, 1903 * On March 22, 1907, designated as {{ill|lt=Shinsenheihakuryokushinsha|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinenheihakuryokushin Shrine}} ; History * The reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Korei|Q313041|WD=Q313041}} (around 290 BC|Q28200}}) Kuroda {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Andomiya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Watakutenhi Hyakusōhime no Mikoto|Q11387098|WD=Q11387098}} settled in Suishunushi at the age of 8 and was praised as the god of free water virtue. * {{ill|lt=Emperor Bunmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} 3rd year (699) {{ill|lt=Dainagon|Q1354706|WD=Q1354706}}{{ill|lt=Ishigamimaro|Q6086140|WD=Q6086140}} dedicates {{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246}} to our company and prays for the end of the epidemic * {{ill|lt=Hoki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}}1st year to 12th year (770-780) Reconstruction of the Gotare ruins * {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}}9 (790) Dengyo Daishi, {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|WD=Q449943}}, at the age of 24, visited Suishinushi Shrine and Daisui Temple.* Around {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} (810-824), Kobo Daishi {{ill|lt=Kukai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}} dug a well of water in the grounds of Suishinushi Shrine and dedicated it to Suishinushi Shrine (it still exists in the grounds as the Well of Suishinushi). * {{ill|lt=Tenryaku|Q1346281|WD=Q1346281}} 2nd year (948) {{ill|lt=Doctor of Literature|Q11500074|WD=Q11500074}} Tachibana Naoki goes as {{ill|lt=Imperial Envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920}} * {{ill|lt=Owa|Q1324768|WD=Q1324768}}2nd year (962){{ill|lt=Council|Q1133663|WD=Q1133663}}{{ill|lt=Tadasuke Fujiwara|Q11623 263|WD=Q11623 263|lt=Fujiwara Asaomi Tadasuke}} worshiped the {{ill|lt=Imperial edict|Q905765|WD=Q905765}} and prayed for rain, and there was a proclamation, and the manor was called Ōsuinushi, and the name was called Ōsuinushi Taisha. * {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}} 1st year (1131) Visit of Sanuki {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Tsunetaka Fujiwara|Q134963520|WD=Q134963520}}* {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q550527|WD=Q550527}}2 years (1132) {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} which was 2 towns, 2 tans and 60 feet became 24 towns and 5 tans. Around this time, there are records that {{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|WD=Q1351234}} and {{ill|lt=Mokudai|Q11581544|WD=Q11581544}} frequently visited the shrine. It is believed that {{ill|lt=Temple territory|Q11458058|WD=Q11458058|lt=Shrine territory}} was donated in the form of dividing a part of Yoda-go and Irino-go, which were Garyo|Q11421344}}. {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367}} It reached the peak of prosperity with 75 members and 42 {{ill|lt=Sōbo|Q11387615|WD=Q11387615|lt=Sōbo}}.
